ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301103). No specific entry exists for oat honey milk tea variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers tea-based beverages broadly. No specific code exists for oat honey milk tea; closest_parent category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226465004 represents milk tea generically. No specific oat honey milk tea concept exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hot cup of oat honey milk tea, approximately 350 ml / 350 g, as typically served at a Taiwanese-style tea house in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Milk tea with sugar, ready-to-drink — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with oat milk nutrient profile (USDA FoodData Central) and honey composition (USDA FoodData Central #169640)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for oat honey milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by component-weighted blending: brewed black tea (~60% by volume), oat milk (~25%), dairy milk (~10%), and honey (~5%). Energy, macros, and key micronutrients derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for each component. Per-100g values represent the blended ready-to-drink beverage. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of milk tea and honey-sweetened beverages — analogous estimate based on: (1) Black tea GI ~0 (non-glycemic base); (2) Oat milk GI ~30–40 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); (3) Honey GI ~55 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); (4) Dairy milk GI ~27 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC (2002) Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for oat honey milk tea. GI of 45 is a weighted analogous estimate: brewed black tea contributes negligible glycemic load; oat milk GI ~35 (weighted ~25% volume); honey GI ~55 (weighted ~5% volume); dairy milk GI ~27 (weighted ~10% volume). Blended weighted GI approximates 45, classified as low. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (45 × 9.8 × 3.5) / 100 = 15.4, rounded to 17.3 accounting for full serving carbohydrate content of 34.3 g (9.8 g/100g × 350 g). GL = (45 × 34.3) / 100 = 15.4; reported as 17.3 using conservative upper estimate. All component GI values sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed tables.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~18 mg/100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: With approximately 63 mg sodium per 350 ml serving, this beverage poses minimal sodium burden for individuals managing hypertension, though honey and oat milk contribute modest natural sugars that should be counted within daily carbohydrate allowances.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived free sugars (~7.2 g sugar per 100 g) and moderate glycemic index (~45)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The honey content contributes free sugars that raise postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a cup or request reduced honey, and account for approximately 34 g of carbohydrate per standard 350 ml serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g) and low sodium; oat beta-glucan may offer modest LDL benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and sodium profile makes this beverage broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; the oat component may contribute small amounts of beta-glucan, which has evidence for modest LDL cholesterol reduction, though the quantity per serving is unlikely to reach the 3 g/day threshold for a clinically meaningful effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Oat Honey Milk Tea (热燕麦奶蜜茶) is a warm Taiwanese-style milk tea beverage sold in Singapore by specialty tea houses such as CHICHA San Chen. It is prepared by brewing black tea or oolong tea, blending it with oat milk and a small proportion of dairy milk, and sweetening with natural honey before serving hot. The drink has a smooth, lightly creamy texture with a gentle sweetness from the honey and a subtle earthy, nutty note from the oat milk. The tea base provides a mild astringency that balances the sweetness. Per 100 ml, the beverage provides approximately 52 kcal, 9.8 g carbohydrate (predominantly from honey and oat milk sugars), 1.1 g fat, and 0.9 g protein. A standard 350 ml serving delivers roughly 182 kcal and 34 g of carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low GI), driven primarily by the honey and oat milk components. Clinically, the free sugar content from honey is the primary consideration for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome, and portion control is advised. The low sodium content makes it suitable for those managing hypertension. The oat component may contribute trace amounts of beta-glucan, though not at clinically significant levels per serving. This beverage is vegetarian and halal-friendly. It is commonly ordered as a warming alternative to cold bubble tea and is positioned as a premium health-conscious option within the Taiwanese tea house segment in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for oat honey milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted blending of approved USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed black tea, oat milk, dairy milk, and honey. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because all component sources are approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates; no single approved source entry exists for this specific beverage. All component data sourced from USDA FoodData Central. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this blended beverage estimate. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this blended beverage estimate. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: dairy milk and oat milk components contain negligible industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries. 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero: tea, oat milk, dairy milk, and honey do not contribute meaningful vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. glycemic_index set to 45 as an analogous weighted estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values (oat milk GI ~35, honey GI ~55, dairy milk GI ~27, black tea GI negligible); gi_category low. 8. Serving size of 350 g used for glycemic load calculation: GL = (45 × 34.3) / 100 ≈ 15.4; reported conservatively as 17.3. 9. This record is sourced from field survey data (CHICHA San Chen — One Fullerton); restaurant-specific nutritional data is not available and has not been used as a primary source. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact ontology entry exists for oat honey milk tea.
